safe-data-modification-workflow.label: Safe data modification workflow
safe-data-modification-workflow.summary: Learn how to safely update and delete data in SQL by previewing affected rows and using WHERE clauses to avoid accidental data loss.

sketch1.title: Always Use WHERE with UPDATE and DELETE

code-1-preview-select.title: Preview rows before updating
code-1-preview-select.prompt: Write a SQL query to preview all rows in `inventory_items` that have `status` set to 'draft'.
code-1-preview-select.hint: Focus on the concept being tested.

help.concept: Think about the role or idea being tested rather than repeating answer wording.
help.hint_1: Eliminate choices or interpretations that do not match the task.
help.hint_2: Choose the concept that best fits what the exercise is asking you to do.

code-2-safe-update.title: Update only test items
code-2-safe-update.prompt: Write a SQL statement to update the `status` to 'inactive' for all rows in `inventory_items` where `is_test` is 1.
code-2-safe-update.hint: Focus on the SQL task being asked for, not on copying final query text.

help.concept: Build the query from the operation the exercise is testing.
help.hint_1: Think about which clauses or functions are required for the task.
help.hint_2: Construct the query based on what result the exercise expects, not by repeating exact solution wording.

code-3-safe-delete.title: Delete only test items
code-3-safe-delete.prompt: Write a SQL statement to delete all rows from `inventory_items` where `is_test` is 1.
code-3-safe-delete.hint: Focus on the concept being tested.

help.concept: Think about the role or idea being tested rather than repeating answer wording.
help.hint_1: Eliminate choices or interpretations that do not match the task.
help.hint_2: Choose the concept that best fits what the exercise is asking you to do.

multi-1-why-preview.title: Why preview before modifying data?
multi-1-why-preview.prompt: Which of the following are reasons to preview rows before running UPDATE or DELETE statements? Select all that apply.
multi-1-why-preview.hint: Think about what could go wrong if you skip the preview step.

help.concept: Previewing helps you avoid accidental changes and confirms your WHERE clause targets the right rows.
help.hint_1: Previewing can prevent mistakes and data loss.
help.hint_2: It helps you confirm the affected rows and avoid deleting or updating the wrong data.

fill-1-where-required.title: Importance of WHERE clause
fill-1-where-required.prompt: Choose the best value for the first missing blank in the statement.
fill-1-where-required.hint: It's the clause that filters which rows are changed.

help.concept: The the missing term clause limits the rows affected by UPDATE or DELETE statements.
help.hint_1: This clause is used to specify which rows to target.
help.hint_2: Without this clause, every row in the table could be changed or deleted.

sketch-1-preview-before-modify.title: Preview Before Modifying Data
sketch-1-preview-before-modify.bodyMarkdown:
Before running an UPDATE or DELETE statement, it's a best practice to preview the rows that will be affected. You can do this by writing a SELECT query with the same WHERE clause you plan to use. For example, if you want to update all draft items in the `inventory_items` table:

```sql
SELECT * FROM inventory_items WHERE status = 'draft';
```

This shows you which rows will be changed, helping prevent accidental updates or deletions.

sketch-2-where-clause-safety.title: Always Use WHERE with UPDATE and DELETE
sketch-2-where-clause-safety.bodyMarkdown:
Using UPDATE or DELETE without a WHERE clause will affect **all rows** in the table. This can lead to massive, unintended data loss. Always include a WHERE clause to target only the intended rows. For example:

```sql
DELETE FROM inventory_items WHERE is_test = 1;
```

This statement safely deletes only test items, not your entire inventory.

sketch-3-safe-update-example.title: Safe Update Example
sketch-3-safe-update-example.bodyMarkdown:
Suppose you want to mark all test items as inactive. First, preview the target rows:

```sql
SELECT id, name, status FROM inventory_items WHERE is_test = 1;
```

Then, run the update:

```sql
UPDATE inventory_items SET status = 'inactive' WHERE is_test = 1;
```

Previewing first helps you confirm exactly which rows will be changed.
